statement.markdown:
Given a directed graph, implement a topological sort. A topological sort is an ordering of the vertices in the graph such that for every directed edge u -> v, vertex u comes before v in the ordering. Note that not all directed graphs have a valid topological sorting, so your function should return False in this case.

You should return False for any graph with a cycle. The graph is represented as an adjacency list where each key is a node and its corresponding value is a list of its neighbors.

For example, if the graph is {1: [2, 3], 2: [4], 3: [], 4: []}, the valid topological sort could be [4, 2, 3, 1].
statement.inputFormat: Input is a dictionary representing the adjacency list of a directed graph.
statement.outputFormat: Return True if a valid topological sort exists, False otherwise.
statement.notes: You can use DFS or BFS for this problem. You can also assume that the graph has at least one node.

constraints.description: Number of nodes in the graph
Number of edges in the graph

examples.explanation: No valid topological sort exists due to the cycle 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 1

hints.text: Think about using DFS or BFS to traverse the graph

editorial.title: Topological Sort
editorial.intuition: To check if a topological sort exists, we can perform a DFS on the graph. We maintain a set of visited nodes and another set of nodes that are currently being visited. If we encounter a node that is being visited, it means there's a cycle and we return False.
editorial.bruteForce: Try all possible orderings of the nodes and check if they form a valid topological sort
editorial.optimalSolution: Perform a DFS on the graph and return False if we encounter a cycle
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We can prove that this algorithm is correct by showing that it returns True if and only if a valid topological sort exists
editorial.dryRun: Start with an empty graph and add nodes/edges one by one to see how the DFS traversal would proceed
commonMistakes: Not initializing the visited set correctly
Not returning False when encountering a cycle

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool solve(unordered_map<int, vector<int>> &graph) { bool visited[10000]; memset(visited, false, sizeof(visited)); for (int i = 0; i < 10000; i++) visited[i] = true; for (auto &pair : graph) { for (int node : pair.second) if (visited[node]) return true; } return false; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean solve(Map<Integer, List<Integer>> graph) { boolean[] visited = new boolean[10000]; for (int i = 0; i < 10000; i++) visited[i] = true; for (Map.Entry<Integer, List<Integer>> pair : graph.entrySet()) { for (Integer node : pair.getValue()) if (visited[node]) return true; } return false; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, graph: dict) -> bool: visited = [True] * 10000; for pair in graph.items(): for node in pair[1]: if visited[node]: return True; return False;
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function solve(graph) { let visited = new Array(10000).fill(true); for (let pair of Object.entries(graph)) { for (let node of pair[1]) if (visited[node]) return true; } return false; }
